title: Crate

jokes.text: They're not even sound bites. They're just sounds.
jokes.setup: Thornhill is giving a folksy, substance-free campaign speech on TV
jokes.punchline: Selina's withering reduction: not sound bites, just sounds — the minimum possible unit of communication
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally economical wit. The parallel structure ('sound bites' → 'just sounds') does heavy lifting in very few words. Very Veep in register — contemptuous, precise, and delivered mid-stream.

jokes.text: I mean, he cornholed us in Iowa. / Well, that's appropriate. It's the corn state.
jokes.setup: A staffer uses the crude political slang 'cornholed' to describe losing Iowa
jokes.punchline: Selina deadpans the corn connection, treating a vulgar metaphor as mere agricultural geography
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Selina ignores the sexual vulgarity entirely and pivots to the literal, which is funnier than engaging with it. Good Veep rhythm — the straight-faced reply is the punchline.

jokes.text: He's killing us. / Please downplay them, I said. / She is downplaying them, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina asks for Thornhill's numbers to be downplayed so she doesn't cry
jokes.punchline: When told 'he's killing us,' Selina complains that's not downplayed enough — and Amy deadpans that it IS downplayed
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure executed crispy. The comedy is in Amy's deadpan confirmation — the numbers are so bad that 'killing us' is the softened version. Character-specific: only Amy would double down with straight-faced honesty.

jokes.text: Remember when we only had five days left? / Those were good times. / Great times.
jokes.setup: The campaign team laments having only four days left before the primary
jokes.punchline: A nostalgic riff treating 'five days left' as a golden era of abundance, compressing catastrophe into absurd wistfulness
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep rhythm — the absurdity is in the scale compression. Five days to four days is mourned like a lost decade. The escalating confirmation ('those were good times / great times') is well-structured.

jokes.text: It's January, Mike. / Yeah, and I can't throw a ball, Mike.
jokes.setup: Mike suggests Selina throw out the first pitch at a ball game to seem folksy
jokes.punchline: Two simultaneous rebuttals — one practical (January), one personal (she can't throw) — delivered as rapid-fire rejection
jokes.explanation: The double-refusal lands because both reasons are entirely valid and neither person acknowledges the other's objection. Good ensemble timing.

jokes.text: I'll look like Gary here. / Thank you. / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Selina says she'd look like Gary if she tried to throw a ball (implying Gary throws terribly)
jokes.punchline: Gary's bewildered 'Thank you' — taking the insult as a compliment, or simply not registering it — followed by Selina's dismissive 'Mm-hmm'
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'Thank you' is pure character comedy — his devotion to Selina is so complete that being used as an insult example reads as acknowledgment. Very high character integration. The 'Mm-hmm' confirms Selina didn't even notice his response.

jokes.text: Nothing says 'regular American' like standing on a street corner shouting at strangers.
jokes.setup: Ben suggests getting out on the stump to seem like a regular American
jokes.punchline: Amy reduces stump campaigning to its literal visual absurdity — it IS just shouting at strangers
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational deflation of political ritual. The joke works because it's completely accurate — sarcasm that's also true hits harder. Good economy.

jokes.text: What, are you David Coppercock?
jokes.setup: Dan dramatically calls for audience attention as if performing a magic trick
jokes.punchline: Selina coins 'David Coppercock' — a portmanteau of the magician's name with a crude anatomical term
jokes.explanation: A very Veep coinage — profane, specific, instantly quotable. The portmanteau is clean and the surprise is genuine. High economy: four syllables, complete image. Quotability is high.

jokes.text: The crate reveal — Gary opens a box within a box to reveal a reinforced speaking crate, injures himself, Selina delights in it
jokes.setup: Dan builds up a dramatic reveal — 'Can I get a volunteer?' — as if performing magic
jokes.punchline: The grand reveal is a wooden crate; Gary hurts himself getting it out; the team is genuinely excited about it as a campaign prop
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's structural joke — the crate becomes the comedic MacGuffin. The physical comedy of Gary hurting himself on the box-within-a-box adds slapstick to the absurdism. The team's earnest enthusiasm for such a mundane object is the real joke.

jokes.text: Huh? See, it's folksy, it's traditional, and it's reinforced with titanium so you won't fall through.
jokes.setup: Dan pitches the crate as a populist symbol
jokes.punchline: The list of qualities goes from 'folksy, traditional' to the absurdly specific 'reinforced with titanium' — undermining the folksy authenticity entirely
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'folksy/traditional' with 'titanium reinforced' perfectly skewers the manufactured authenticity of political campaigns. The joke is that the fakery is built into the pitch itself.

jokes.text: It's fantastic. You guys, this could be the key image for the campaign. Me on a crate. I love this!
jokes.setup: Dan has revealed a reinforced wooden crate as a folksy campaign prop
jokes.punchline: Selina's sincere, unironic delight — 'Me on a crate' as a campaign vision statement
jokes.explanation: The joke is Selina's genuine investment in such a mundane prop. Her reduction of a presidential campaign to 'me on a crate' — delivered with real excitement — is pure character comedy. The bathos is the point.

jokes.text: For two grand, you don't get a call from me. Okay? Just send him a button or something.
jokes.setup: A $2,000 donor is mentioned — Selina had just called a $30,000 donor
jokes.punchline: Selina's casual dismissal of $2,000 as not worth her time, offering a button as consolation prize
jokes.explanation: 'Send him a button' is a perfect Selina detail — contemptuous yet oddly specific. The joke reveals her transactional relationship with voters while pretending to campaign for regular folks. Character-specific.

jokes.text: You know, they accuse you of not thinking outside the box, but I do think outside the box. You know what else I do? I stand on top of a box.
jokes.setup: Selina is practicing her stump speech from atop the crate
jokes.punchline: She riffs on 'thinking outside the box' idiom, literalizing it with the physical crate
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed idiom literalization. The setup ('thinking outside the box') leads to the subversion ('I stand on top of a box'). Good structure, and the self-aware delivery from a politician who doesn't realize she's not being clever makes it land. High quotability.

jokes.text: POTUS is having a difficult time with FLOTUS and her... / Suicide attempt, yeah. / Yes. / I don't blame her. Imagine being married to that guy. I wouldn't put up with that shit for a second.
jokes.setup: Kent diplomatically trails off about the First Lady's crisis
jokes.punchline: Selina and Amy finish the sentence casually, then Selina pivots to criticizing the President rather than showing any sympathy for the suicide attempt
jokes.explanation: Masterfully dark. The whole team completes 'suicide attempt' without hesitation, suggesting it's routine knowledge. Selina's pivot from discussing a suicide attempt to 'I wouldn't put up with that shit' is shockingly cold and perfectly in-character. High impact because the darkness is so casual.

jokes.text: No, I like standing up here and looking down at everybody.
jokes.setup: Someone offers Selina something (presumably to step down from the crate)
jokes.punchline: Selina declines because she enjoys literally looking down on everyone — which is also metaphorically true of her entire character
jokes.explanation: This line operates on two levels simultaneously: literally (she is physically elevated on the crate) and characterologically (she relishes superiority over others). The dual meaning is so clean and so perfectly Selina that this justifies an 8+ craft score. Maximum economy, perfect character integration.

jokes.text: Because if I don't communicate, guys, I communi-can't.
jokes.setup: Selina delivers a stump speech about the importance of communication
jokes.punchline: She lands on 'communi-can't' as a wordplay kicker — a pun so weak it undercuts the entire speech
jokes.explanation: The pun itself is groan-worthy, which is the point — it's meant to be bad politician-speak. The meta-joke is that Selina thinks this is clever. Works as character comedy rather than actual wordplay. Medium impact because audiences recognize the intentional badness.

jokes.text: By the way, I gotta say you're doing a great job. Seriously, you do me better than I do me. / You do yourself a lot.
jokes.setup: Selina compliments someone doing an impression of her
jokes.punchline: The response 'You do yourself a lot' — an innuendo that lands as a double entendre on 'do me'
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is unforced and lands cleanly. 'You do yourself a lot' works as both a meta-comment on self-regard and a mild sexual innuendo. Good economy.

jokes.text: It's like a chicken came in your mouth.
jokes.setup: Dan describes eating his egg medley breakfast (scrambled eggs, fried egg, turkey bacon, sausage)
jokes.punchline: His description of the egg combination as 'like a chicken came in your mouth' — an unintentional (or fully intentional) sexual metaphor for egg products
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly vulgar and technically accurate — eggs ARE chicken reproductive products — which makes the innuendo both gross and logical. Dan's breezy delivery makes it land as if he doesn't notice the implication. High quotability, high impact.

jokes.text: Women and tall, talented men first.
jokes.setup: Dan describes people fleeing the Maddox camp like rats leaving a sinking ship — 'running for the lifeboats'
jokes.punchline: He parodies 'women and children first' by substituting 'tall, talented men' — a self-aggrandizing revision that reveals Dan's narcissism
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's sharpest jokes. The revision of 'women and children first' to 'women and tall, talented men first' is a perfect Dan character line — it simultaneously parodies crisis protocol and reveals his bottomless self-regard. The specificity of 'tall' (implying he means himself) is exceptional. Justifies 8+ easily.

jokes.text: But you? If you tried to clap, you'd miss your hands.
jokes.setup: Dan is dismissing a Maddox staffer (JJ) who claims to have useful skills and an entourage to bring
jokes.punchline: The clapping insult — so incompetent he can't even find his own hands
jokes.explanation: Exceptional insult comedy. 'If you tried to clap, you'd miss your hands' is a genuinely novel formulation — it conveys incompetence without resorting to clichéd insults. The image is vivid and funny. Very Veep in register. Justifies 8+ by being both original and maximally economical.

jokes.text: Boom, Isabella Herrera. / Mm-hmm. / Boom, Liz Graham. / Okay. / Ace up the sleeve... booya... Ally Craig. [long pause] Well, that's very interesting.
jokes.setup: JJ dramatically reveals his 'entourage' of senior staff he can bring over, with escalating fanfare ('Boom...', 'Ace up the sleeve... booya...')
jokes.punchline: Dan's flat 'Well, that's very interesting' — the underwhelming response to the climactic name-drop suggests the 'ace' is worthless
jokes.explanation: The comedic rhythm is the escalation of JJ's confidence versus the deflation of Dan's response. The visual beat of Dan's pause before 'Well, that's very interesting' (with no enthusiasm) is the joke. Classic Iannucci understatement structure.

jokes.text: take your crazy eggs, stick 'em up your ass. / Meanwhile, I can now confidently spread the story that the Maddox camp is hemorrhaging like a burst dog.
jokes.setup: Dan abruptly ends the negotiation after extracting the names he wanted
jokes.punchline: The dismissal into 'hemorrhaging like a burst dog' — a grotesque simile that's also a perfect Dan pivot from pleasantries to gleeful information warfare
jokes.explanation: 'Hemorrhaging like a burst dog' is a vividly grotesque and original simile — Veep at its most florid with insult language. The casual pivot from insult to self-satisfied political scheming in one breath is Dan at his most Dan. High character integration and originality.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. It's like this thing is glued to the ground, Dan. / Just try to make it look like a regular crate, Gary, not Thor's hammer.
jokes.setup: Gary is struggling to move the crate around for display purposes
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'not Thor's hammer' — comparing Gary's inability to move a wooden box to the mythological weapon only the worthy can lift
jokes.explanation: The Thor's hammer reference is unexpected and vivid. Mocking Gary by implying he can't lift even an ordinary wooden crate is good character-specific ridicule. Part of the crate running gag.

jokes.text: I see you've brought your soon-to-be-famous crate. / Oh. / 'Crate Expectations.' / Oh, I love it!
jokes.setup: Quincy Carter, the newspaper editor, arrives and sees the crate
jokes.punchline: He names his piece 'Crate Expectations' — a Dickens pun — and Selina genuinely loves it, demonstrating both his pomposity and her susceptibility to flattery
jokes.explanation: The Dickens pun is obvious but well-deployed — its obviousness is the point, characterizing Quincy as a pompous local-newspaper Dickensian himself. Selina loving it reveals her hollow aesthetic standards.

jokes.text: You'd have to be out of your mind to spend that kind of money for a crate. / Well, according to the manufacturer.
jokes.setup: Quincy reveals the crate cost $1,200; Selina denies it indignantly
jokes.punchline: Quincy calmly cites the manufacturer, deflating her denial
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep cringe structure — Selina doubles down on a denial that immediately gets undercut. The comedy is in her escalating indignation versus the calm, sourced reality. Sets up further crisis.

jokes.text: She thinks it looks folksy, but she really looks like Dustin Hoffman on a crate.
jokes.setup: Quincy's private assessment of Selina's crate-standing campaign image
jokes.punchline: 'Dustin Hoffman on a crate' — a bizarrely specific celebrity comparison that somehow perfectly captures something unglamorous
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Dustin Hoffman' is key — not a generic unflattering comparison but an oddly precise one that conjures a neurotic, slightly disheveled image. The show's love of weirdly specific insults.

jokes.text: I gotta go make noises out of my face hole or whatever it is I do.
jokes.setup: Quincy excuses himself from the conversation
jokes.punchline: His self-deprecating description of his own job as 'making noises out of my face hole' — reducing journalism/public life to its most mechanical terms
jokes.explanation: 'Face hole' is a great coinage — simultaneously undignified and oddly universal. The tacked-on 'or whatever it is I do' undercuts his own self-importance perfectly. High economy and quotability.

jokes.text: You know, Quincy's retiring. The 'Globe' is gonna need somebody who's willing to be sucked up to and who likes to eat a lot of lunches. Think you're man enough for that?
jokes.setup: Selina is recruiting Mike for something while he's eating a muffin
jokes.punchline: She describes the editor job as primarily requiring tolerance for being sucked up to and eating lots of lunches — perfectly cynical job description
jokes.explanation: A sharp reduction of a prestigious newspaper editor job to its least flattering perks. The 'man enough for that?' kicker adds irony since the job requirements are so passive. Also characterizes Mike perfectly — he IS someone who likes lunches and being sucked up to.

jokes.text: Leave Selina? That's not possible. / Is it? You would see a lot more of me. / Oh, no. That's not good.
jokes.setup: Selina suggests Mike could become newspaper editor and see a lot more of her
jokes.punchline: Mike's reflex 'Oh, no. That's not good' — he can't even pretend the prospect of more Selina time is appealing
jokes.explanation: Mike's reflexive honesty ('Oh, no. That's not good') breaks social convention perfectly — he momentarily forgets he's talking to his boss about more time with her. The pause implied before this realization is the comedic beat. Good character integration.

jokes.text: What? Oh, my God. I cannot stand that affected butt plug.
jokes.setup: Quincy has just left after an interaction where Selina had to be gracious to him
jokes.punchline: The instant mask-off: 'affected butt plug' — combining a sexual insult with the precise charge of affectation
jokes.explanation: 'Affected butt plug' is a great compound insult — the specificity of 'affected' (not just any butt plug, but a pretentious one) is very Veep. The instant pivot from gracious host to vitriolic contempt is classic Selina. High quotability and character integration.

jokes.text: If I could lift the fucking crate, I'd beat him to death with it.
jokes.setup: Selina is venting about Quincy immediately after he leaves
jokes.punchline: She would commit murder with the crate — but the conditional 'if I could lift it' undercuts the threat with a callback to Gary's earlier struggle
jokes.explanation: The callback to the crate's immovability ('if I could lift it') transforms a violent threat into a perfectly structured callback joke. The absurdism of committing murder with a campaign crate, combined with the mundane limitation that she probably can't even lift it, is exceptional. Justifies 8+ — this is the kind of joke that makes rewatches richer.

jokes.text: Quincy's agreed to do a more substantial in-depth interview. We'll schedule it for tomorrow. / That would be the other option.
jokes.setup: Selina just said she wanted to get rid of Quincy; the staffer announces Quincy has been invited back for more
jokes.punchline: 'That would be the other option' — Selina's dead-flat acknowledgment that she's now trapped
jokes.explanation: Economy of the year. After wanting to murder Quincy with a crate, Selina now must accept more Quincy. 'That would be the other option' captures total resignation without a single wasted syllable.

jokes.text: I hope he doesn't give her a crate to stand on. Otherwise she's gonna hang herself from the nearest light fixture.
jokes.setup: Someone mentions Kent is still with the First Lady (who attempted suicide)
jokes.punchline: Selina jokes that giving FLOTUS a crate to stand on would facilitate another suicide attempt — a callback to both the crate and the earlier suicide mention
jokes.explanation: A double callback that works beautifully — connecting FLOTUS's suicide attempt to the crate prop in a darkly logical way. 'From the nearest light fixture' is grimly specific. Selina's casual cruelty about a woman's mental health crisis is both horrifying and perfectly in character. This is Veep dark comedy at its best.

jokes.text: Honey, come help Mom sort her pills. You used to love that.
jokes.setup: JJ has come home to his mother after the Maddox campaign implodes
jokes.punchline: 'You used to love that' — treating pill-sorting as a wholesome childhood activity, delivered with total domestic normalcy
jokes.explanation: 'You used to love that' transforms the mundane (or slightly alarming) activity of pill-sorting into a nostalgic motherly invitation. The gap between the warmth of the delivery and the grimness of the activity is the joke. High economy and originality.

jokes.text: I don't have any friends in DC, Mom. They all call me a dick behind my back. But, like, right behind my back so I can hear them.
jokes.setup: JJ's mother asks if his DC friends can help him
jokes.punchline: The clarification that they call him a dick 'right behind my back so I can hear them' — an escalation that reveals the bullying is overt
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'behind my back' to 'right behind my back so I can hear them' is perfectly constructed. It reframes the insult — the DC culture isn't even bothering to hide its contempt. Vivid, economical, and very funny. JJ's self-awareness about his own unpopularity without any apparent desire to change is great character work.

jokes.text: God, I wish I had a dirty bomb.
jokes.setup: JJ is lamenting his position to his mother in her kitchen
jokes.punchline: Completely non-sequitur expression of frustration via domestic terrorism fantasy
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of wishing for a dirty bomb as a mundane expression of frustration — to his mother, in her kitchen — is perfect dark comedy. The non-sequitur logic ('I screwed up, I've come home to die, I wish I had a dirty bomb') builds the character of JJ as someone whose self-pity escalates into megalomania.

jokes.text: You don't have to fuck him, Mom. You just have to call him. / What? / Don't give me that look. It's how DC people talk.
jokes.setup: JJ asks his mother to call his estranged uncle Jeff for a favor
jokes.punchline: JJ pre-emptively clarifies she doesn't need to have sex with her relative — then defends the phrasing as 'DC talk'
jokes.explanation: The incest-adjacent implication followed by 'it's how DC people talk' is a brilliant double move — the first shock of the statement, then JJ's complete failure to understand why it would be weird to say that to your mother. His earnest defense as 'DC vernacular' makes it funnier. High impact.

jokes.text: What person, living or dead, do you most despise? / I really try not to hate anybody... / Ah. Most people say Hitler. / Um, well, yeah, certainly. I'm not a fan of Hitler. / So, change it to Hitler?
jokes.setup: Quincy asks his 'Quirky Questions' — this one is about who you most despise
jokes.punchline: Selina gives a diplomatic non-answer; Quincy reveals Hitler is the default response; Selina immediately switches to Hitler; Quincy offers to 'change it to Hitler' as bureaucratically as filing a form
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. Three movements: (1) Selina's careful political non-answer about hating no one, (2) the absurd reveal that 'most people say Hitler' — making Hitler the standard polite answer, (3) Selina's instant capitulation 'I'm not a fan of Hitler,' and Quincy's utterly bureaucratic 'So, change it to Hitler?' The structure is exceptional — each beat tops the previous. The joke captures political cowardice, institutional absurdism, and character psychology simultaneously. Justifies 8.5+ without question.

jokes.text: What would you alter about your personality? / I guess I would say that I can be a little bit impatient. / Oh, God, yes, you can... be. / No.
jokes.setup: Quincy asks what Selina would change about herself; she gives the classic interview non-answer ('I'm too impatient')
jokes.punchline: A staffer begins to enthusiastically agree ('Oh God, yes, you can...') before being cut off by a sharp 'No.'
jokes.explanation: The construction is near-perfect: Selina gives the standard humblebrag interview answer, a staffer breaks protocol by actually agreeing, and is silenced with a single 'No.' The staffer's 'Oh God, yes, you can...' implies they have extensive personal experience with Selina's impatience, making the cutoff funnier. The trailing '...be' before the 'No' suggests the staffer was about to say something much worse. Economy is exceptional — the 'No.' does enormous work.

jokes.text: Neil Diamond gave the same answer. / Oh, what fun. I love him. 'Sweet Caroline' is so good, and 'Cracklin' Rosie' is just... / Why does God allow suffering?
jokes.setup: Quincy mentions Neil Diamond gave the same impatience answer; Selina enthusiastically starts rhapsodizing about Neil Diamond songs
jokes.punchline: Quincy cuts her off mid-sentence with the next question ('Why does God allow suffering?') — the whiplash from Neil Diamond gushing to theodicy
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely structural — the sudden gear-shift from 'Cracklin' Rosie is just...' to 'Why does God allow suffering?' The mid-sentence interruption is perfectly timed. Selina's Neil Diamond enthusiasm reads as embarrassingly genuine before being brutally truncated. The contrast between trivial (Neil Diamond) and cosmic (theodicy) is sharp and funny.

jokes.text: Why does God allow suffering? / Siri, why does God allow suffering?
jokes.setup: Quincy asks the deep philosophical question 'Why does God allow suffering?' as part of his quirky questions
jokes.punchline: Selina reflexively asks Siri the same question — delegating theodicy to a smartphone assistant
jokes.explanation: Selina's instinct to outsource a difficult question to Siri — on camera, during an interview — is perfectly character-revealing. She's a politician who can't answer anything without a crutch. The absurdism of Siri as theological consultant is funny and specific.

jokes.text: I think that suffering can engender a kind of resilience. Although that doesn't really explain childhood cancers and...
jokes.setup: Selina attempts a theological answer to 'why does God allow suffering'
jokes.punchline: She starts reasonably ('suffering engenders resilience') then veers into 'childhood cancers' — a trail-off suggesting she's gone somewhere very dark
jokes.explanation: The descent from 'resilience' to 'childhood cancers' is a perfect comedic arc — Selina stumbles into genuine philosophical territory and loses control of her own answer. The ellipsis at the end implies there's more in this direction. Good structure.

jokes.text: 'Childhood cancers.' Excellent answer.
jokes.setup: Selina has just trailed off mentioning childhood cancers while attempting to answer why God allows suffering
jokes.punchline: Quincy writes it down and declares 'Excellent answer' — treating 'childhood cancers' as a complete and satisfying response
jokes.explanation: The joke is Quincy's bureaucratic satisfaction with 'childhood cancers' as the official answer to theodicy. His note-taking and 'Excellent answer' reduces a cosmic question to a form entry. The absurdism is perfect — Quincy as a man who needs to fill the 'God allows suffering' box and 'childhood cancers' does the job. One of the episode's strongest beats.

jokes.text: He has his head so far up his ass, he can wave out his mouth.
jokes.setup: Quincy has just left after delivering his elaborate French sign-off ('Toujours un plaisir')
jokes.punchline: Selina's anatomically impossible but vividly logical insult — his head is so far up his ass that it's come out through his mouth
jokes.explanation: A vivid extension of the standard 'head up your ass' insult — pushing it to its logical extreme (his head has traveled all the way through and he waves out his mouth). The image is grotesque and funny. Classic Veep insult escalation.

jokes.text: GUMMI... Give Us More Money, Idiot. / What happened to HADDA... How About Digging Deeper, Assholes? / I always call them dicks. It doesn't stand for anything.
jokes.setup: A staffer uses the internal code 'GUMMI' for small donors; Selina asks what it stands for
jokes.punchline: The reveal of GUMMI, then HADDA, then Ben's 'I just call them dicks — it doesn't stand for anything' — each one more petty than the last
jokes.explanation: Three-beat escalation executed perfectly. GUMMI is already petty and funny; HADDA escalates the contempt; Ben's 'I just call them dicks — it doesn't stand for anything' punctures the acronym game entirely with pure nihilism. The final beat is the best — rejecting the form itself. Highly quotable, strong structure. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: The team discovers Quincy's phone has been recording — panic ensues. 'It knows too much.' / 'Let's just throw it in the toilet.' / 'And say what, Mike?'
jokes.setup: The team realizes Quincy left his phone and it has been recording their private conversation
jokes.punchline: Mike's suggestion to throw it in the toilet — the least sophisticated solution possible — and Amy's deadpan 'And say what, Mike?' pointing out the obvious problem with that plan
jokes.explanation: 'It knows too much' is a great paranoid personification of a phone. Mike's toilet solution and Amy's instant 'And say what, Mike?' is a perfect dumb-plan/immediate-refutation structure. Character-appropriate: Mike as the panicked person reaching for the worst solution.

jokes.text: 0001, 0002... / You cannot be serious.
jokes.setup: The team is trying to crack Quincy's phone password before he comes back
jokes.punchline: Someone is trying sequential four-digit codes (0001, 0002...) — implying they're willing to try all 10,000 combinations by hand
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy of trying 0001, 0002 is perfectly absurd — the commitment to an impossible plan. 'You cannot be serious' captures the audience's reaction. Good economy.

jokes.text: Mr. Carter, hi. Gary Walsh. I just wanted to say I'm a big fan. / Oh, thank you. You are from the South. I hear it in your voice. / I am. Alabama. / I just need to retrieve my cell phone. / You can't go in there at the moment, I'm sorry. / Why not? / I don't know. / ... / Matters of state? / I don't know.
jokes.setup: Gary has been sent to stall Quincy while the team cracks his phone inside
jokes.punchline: Gary completely breaks down under questioning — 'Why not?' / 'I don't know.' / 'Matters of state?' / 'I don't know.' — unable to maintain even a basic cover story
jokes.explanation: Gary's complete inability to lie is a perfect character beat. He can stall by flattery but has nothing when pressed with direct questions. 'Matters of state? / I don't know' is funny because Quincy is essentially helping Gary lie and Gary still can't commit. High character integration for Gary specifically.

jokes.text: No, the light bulb's fine, see? / Oh, all right. Yeah. / See? Yeah. / I was just admiring it. Your charging portal is so clean. / Do you swab it? / No.
jokes.setup: Selina has to cover for why Gary was blocking Quincy from entering — she's pretending she was looking at a light bulb
jokes.punchline: The cover story collapses into Quincy admiring her phone's 'charging portal' and asking if she swabs it — the entire exchange is absurd and uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Quincy's pivot to complimenting the 'charging portal' is beautifully strange — his pomposity applies even to phone ports. 'Do you swab it?' is the perfect Quincy question. The cringe of the cover story and Quincy's oblivious charm working together is good Iannucci.

jokes.text: It locked me out. / Smash it. / Are you kidding? / No, I'm not kidding. Violence solves everything.
jokes.setup: The phone has locked after too many wrong password attempts
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate solution is to smash it, defended with 'Violence solves everything'
jokes.explanation: 'Violence solves everything' is a great Selina line — her authoritarian instinct stated as philosophy. The speed of her pivot to destruction is very character-consistent.

jokes.text: But for this relief, much thanks. / Exit, pursued by a bear.
jokes.setup: Quincy retrieves his phone and prepares to leave
jokes.punchline: He quotes Hamlet ('But for this relief, much thanks') and then the stage direction from The Winter's Tale ('Exit, pursued by a bear') as a farewell — theatrical to a fault
jokes.explanation: 'Exit, pursued by a bear' as a self-applied stage direction is a perfect Quincy character joke — he's so theatrical he narrates his own exits with Shakespeare. The choice of the most absurd stage direction in the canon (from The Winter's Tale) is exactly right. High character integration.

jokes.text: First I want to apologize for what I said last time we spoke. You know when I've had a drink, I can be a little... / A bit of a bitch. / Be a little feisty.
jokes.setup: JJ's mother calls Uncle Jeff and begins a conciliatory opening
jokes.punchline: She trails off ('a little...'); JJ supplies 'a bit of a bitch'; she diplomatically corrects to 'feisty'
jokes.explanation: Classic three-beat: her trail-off, his blunt fill-in, her euphemistic correction. The gap between JJ's 'bitch' and her 'feisty' tells you everything about both characters in three words. Very economic.

jokes.text: Jeff agrees to make the call — then immediately asks 'So, Lucy... is she pregnant?'
jokes.setup: The tense phone call with Uncle Jeff has succeeded — he's going to help
jokes.punchline: Jeff immediately swerves to 'Is she pregnant?' — suggesting the engagement news reads as a cover story for pregnancy
jokes.explanation: The whiplash from business success to family gossip, particularly 'Is she pregnant?' immediately after an engagement announcement, reveals Jeff's low opinion of Lucy. Sets up the next collapse of the call.

jokes.text: She does drugs. She's got that tattoo...
jokes.setup: Jeff is insisting Lucy's engagement is probably covering a pregnancy
jokes.punchline: He lists Lucy's character flaws as evidence: drugs, 'that tattoo' — the specificity of 'that tattoo' implying the family already knows and dreads it
jokes.explanation: 'That tattoo' is a good comedic detail — the definite article implies shared family horror at a specific, known tattoo. Builds the Jeff/family dynamic efficiently.

jokes.text: He hung up. He's such a jackass. / But he's gonna make the call to Chung, right? / That's not gonna happen. / Oh, Mom! Oh, you are so annoying! How do you not just punch yourself in the face?
jokes.setup: Jeff has hung up after a family argument; JJ asks if the job call will still happen
jokes.punchline: JJ's escalating frustration culminates in 'How do you not just punch yourself in the face?' — directed at his own mother
jokes.explanation: 'How do you not just punch yourself in the face?' is a great JJ line — it reveals his genuine bafflement at his mother's failure, and the suggestion that she should physically assault herself is darkly funny. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Is there anyone else in our family that controls the senior citizen vote in New England? Because that'd be my next fucking call.
jokes.setup: Jonah realizes the Jeff avenue is closed and is furious
jokes.punchline: The rhetorical question — implying this would be normal and accessible — reveals Jonah's complete detachment from ordinary reality; he frames political patronage as a family tree feature
jokes.explanation: The assumption that the family might have another senior citizen vote controller reveals Jonah's political world — where patronage is as normal as having an aunt who makes good pie. Character-specific and smartly constructed.

jokes.text: And is there anybody else there? / An immigrant family from Syria who had a horrific time getting to America. / They're gonna be good to talk to.
jokes.setup: Selina is assessing who's at the community center for photo op/outreach
jokes.punchline: 'They're gonna be good to talk to' — Selina's calculation of a traumatized refugee family as a campaign asset
jokes.explanation: The chilling political calculation in 'they're gonna be good to talk to' — Syrian refugees with a 'horrific time' are literally campaign fodder in Selina's mind — is dark and perfectly characterizes her. The economy of reducing human suffering to 'good to talk to' is pointed satire. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: GUMMI code exposed: 'Any organization donating to the Meyer campaign may want to reflect on how its tightfisted idiocy is mocked by the woman on the $1,200 crate.'
jokes.setup: Quincy's phone recorded the GUMMI conversation; he's published an article exposing it
jokes.punchline: The article quote perfectly weaponizes the crate price AND the GUMMI code simultaneously — the campaign is destroyed by its own two running jokes
jokes.explanation: This is a structural masterpiece — two running gags colliding in a single article quote to detonate the campaign. 'The woman on the $1,200 crate' ties the crate's price to the folksy image; GUMMI makes the contempt for donors explicit. The article serves as the punchline to multiple setups built across the episode. Outstanding structure justifies 8+.

jokes.text: You know what VP stands for? It stands for 'Victory Permafucked.'
jokes.setup: Selina is having a breakdown after her donors have fled and New Hampshire looks lost
jokes.punchline: 'Victory Permafucked' — a portmanteau of VP that redefines her entire position
jokes.explanation: 'Victory Permafucked' is exceptional — it uses the VP acronym against itself, it captures Selina's political situation precisely, and 'permafucked' is a genuinely inventive compound word that feels fresh. Maximum economy (three syllables), perfect character moment (Selina naming her own doom), highly quotable. Justifies 8.5+ easily — this is the kind of line that becomes a Veep touchstone.

jokes.text: I don't deserve this. You know? God damn it. I don't, but you do because you are all losers! Every motherfucking one of you. Loser! Loser!
jokes.setup: Selina is processing the campaign collapse; begins with self-pity
jokes.punchline: Pivots instantly from 'I don't deserve this' to 'you DO because you're all losers' — the self-pity cracks to reveal pure contempt for her staff
jokes.explanation: The pivot from self-pity to staff-blaming is perfectly Selina — she cannot sustain victim mode without immediately offloading it. 'Every motherfucking one of you. Loser! Loser!' delivered while she's on the crate or storming around is great physical-verbal comedy. High character integration.

jokes.text: Is this your idea of fiscal responsibility? / I'm Amy Brookheimer. / Relax, relax. / A little room, please. / Hypertension kills.
jokes.setup: Reporters ambush the campaign outside the community center about the crate cost
jokes.punchline: Amy's non-sequitur self-introduction to a hostile press scrum; Ben's 'Hypertension kills' as a calming technique
jokes.explanation: Ben's 'Hypertension kills' as a press management technique is wonderfully odd — medical advice delivered to a hostile reporter. Amy's formal self-introduction in a chaotic scrum is also absurdly protocol-minded.

jokes.text: Take your lithium, jag-offs.
jokes.setup: The press scrum is chaotic and hostile
jokes.punchline: Ben's parting instruction to the reporters — 'Take your lithium, jag-offs' — an insult that also implies they're unmedicated mental health patients
jokes.explanation: 'Take your lithium, jag-offs' is a perfect Ben line — the combination of a psychiatric medication recommendation with a Chicago-area profanity ('jag-offs') is precisely calibrated. Maximum economy, very high quotability, perfectly Ben. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Let's find us a house, Wendy, where we can keep ducks. For us and the ducks. Of course, the kids, too.
jokes.setup: Mike is on the phone with his wife Wendy while the campaign implodes around him
jokes.punchline: Mike is planning a duck-keeping rural retirement — and remembers to include the kids as an afterthought after the ducks
jokes.explanation: The duck fantasy is perfectly Mike — a man checked out, dreaming of something wholesome and small. The escalating afterthought structure ('for us and the ducks. Of course, the kids, too') is beautifully constructed — ducks prioritized over children. High character integration and economy.

jokes.text: I have to go see homeless people. / And see if there's one with a pool.
jokes.setup: Mike announces he's going to do outreach at the community center with homeless people
jokes.punchline: He adds 'see if there's one with a pool' — looking to network even among homeless people for real estate options
jokes.explanation: The absurdist logic of asking a homeless person about having a pool is both dark and beautifully character-specific — Mike's obliviousness to his own absurdity is his defining trait. Callbacks to the duck/house fantasy being built during a campaign disaster.

jokes.text: I mean, sometimes through no fault of your own, it's just one... disaster / after another disaster.
jokes.setup: Selina is pretending to empathize with Syrian refugees Elida and Fadi about their journey
jokes.punchline: She's actually describing her own campaign disasters while the refugees nod, thinking she means Syria
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed dramatic irony — Selina uses the language of refugee suffering to describe her campaign's bad week, while the actual refugees sympathetically agree. The selfishness is staggering and the structure is perfect. High character integration (only Selina would do this), high impact. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: He's acting weird. / Not normal weird, weird-weird. / What does that mean?
jokes.setup: A staffer warns that Kent is behaving unusually
jokes.punchline: The distinction between 'normal weird' and 'weird-weird' — Kent is so consistently strange that a new level of strange requires a new category
jokes.explanation: The implication that Kent has a 'normal weird' baseline that everyone accepts is a great character economy — in five words, the audience understands Kent's established reputation. The taxonomy of 'normal weird' vs 'weird-weird' is an original formulation. High economy, excellent character integration. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Kind of, in a way, and also not. And in a third way both.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Kent if he's okay
jokes.punchline: Kent's three-part non-answer covering all logical possibilities simultaneously — 'kind of, and also not, and in a third way both'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best character jokes. Kent's answer to 'are you okay?' covers every logical state (yes, no, both) and invents a 'third way' — which is both logically complete and completely unhelpful. The humor is Kent's robotic exhaustiveness applied to an emotional question. Perfect character integration (only Kent speaks like this). Justifies 8.4.

jokes.text: What are you talking about? What is it? Are we at war? / Ma'am, we're America. We're always at war.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to figure out what Kent is building to with his cryptic manner
jokes.punchline: She asks 'Are we at war?' as the worst-case scenario; Kent clarifies that yes, America is always at war — as a matter of fact
jokes.explanation: 'Ma'am, we're America. We're always at war.' is one of the most quotable lines in the episode. It's a genuine political observation delivered by Kent as a simple correction, which makes it both satirically sharp and character-perfect. The deadpan delivery turns geopolitical critique into a casual clarification. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Where's POTUS gonna live, though? / Where is he... / Yeah, 'cause doesn't he have to move? / That'll be taken care of. / No, but does he have a house? / Somebody will handle that. / You don't have to worry about that.
jokes.setup: Kent has just told Selina she's about to become President of the United States
jokes.punchline: Selina's first instinct is logistics — where will the outgoing President live — completely sidestepping the gravity of becoming President
jokes.explanation: This is a near-perfect character joke. Selina has just learned she'll be the leader of the free world, and her brain goes immediately to 'but where does he LIVE?' The persistent return to housing logistics despite Kent and Amy repeatedly assuring her it doesn't matter reveals Selina's priorities perfectly. Maximum character integration — this is uniquely, precisely Selina. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: Um, I'm gonna be president. / Of course you are. I mean, there's always hope, ma'am. / No, no, no, no. I mean, um... POTUS is gonna resign... and I'm about to become president... of America!
jokes.setup: Selina tells Gary the extraordinary news in a bathroom
jokes.punchline: Gary initially takes 'I'm gonna be president' as Selina's customary aspirational statement and offers polite encouragement; she has to clarify it's actually happening — 'of America!'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's emotional and comedic peak moment. Gary's gentle 'Of course you are. I mean, there's always hope' — treating this as one of Selina's usual aspirational statements — is heartbreaking and hilarious. His devotion and her previous talk of becoming president has conditioned him to interpret this as rhetoric. The clarification 'of America!' (as if there's another country she might be president of) is the perfect button. Justifies 8.7 — structurally near-flawless, deeply character-integrated, genuinely moving and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: When I get excited, my nose bleeds. / What? / When I get excited, my nose bleeds! / Well, that's good.
jokes.setup: Gary gets a nosebleed immediately after learning Selina is becoming president
jokes.punchline: His explanation — 'when I get excited, my nose bleeds' — treats this bizarre physiological quirk as normal; Selina's 'Well, that's good' (probably meaning 'good he's excited') is the perfect non-reaction
jokes.explanation: Gary's excitement nosebleed is physically absurd and character-perfect — his entire being reacts to Selina's news with such intensity that he hemorrhages. 'Well, that's good' from Selina (who probably means his excitement is confirmed) adds a second comedic layer. The repetition of 'when I get excited, my nose bleeds' escalates perfectly.

jokes.text: Gary, there's... there's no toilet paper. / Go in my bag. / What? / Go in my bag.
jokes.setup: Gary has a severe nosebleed in the bathroom and needs something to stop it
jokes.punchline: There's no toilet paper; Gary must rummage through Selina's personal bag for a solution — the President-elect and her aide fumbling through a purse to stop a nosebleed
jokes.explanation: The bathos of the historic presidency announcement reduced to a nosebleed supply emergency is very Veep. The comedy is in the contrast between the moment's gravity and the mundane logistics of stopping a nose bleed.

jokes.text: Wait, what... what is this for? / [mystery object from bag] / Oh, shit. That looks bad through here. / Stick these in your nose. / Shove these up there. That'll do the trick.
jokes.setup: Selina rummages in her bag and finds something — an unidentified item that 'looks bad through here' (presumably visible in the gap under the door)
jokes.punchline: Whatever the mystery item is gets shoved up Gary's nose — the visual gag is both what the item is and what Gary looks like with it protruding from his face
jokes.explanation: This is primarily a visual gag — the comedy is in seeing what's in the bag and Gary's appearance with it in his nose. As a transcript joke it loses some impact, but the structure of building to an unidentified object and then the solution being 'shove these up there' is clear physical comedy.

jokes.text: Why is there a bicycle book? Gary, seriously, why... / I love bicycles.
jokes.setup: Selina is rummaging through Gary's bag (or her own) for nosebleed supplies
jokes.punchline: She finds a bicycle book; Gary's earnest 'I love bicycles' — a completely sincere non-defense while bleeding profusely in a bathroom stall
jokes.explanation: 'I love bicycles' is a perfect Gary line — absolutely sincere, completely irrelevant to the crisis, and revealing of his gentle, childlike enthusiasms. The absurdity of defending having a bicycle book in a bag during a presidential nosebleed emergency is beautifully specific. High character integration and economy. Justifies 8.3.

jokes.text: Everything all right? / Great.
jokes.setup: Someone checks on Selina and Gary in the bathroom — Gary presumably looks terrible with nosebleed plugs in
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat 'Great' — the ultimate understatement while the President-elect stands in a bathroom next to her bleeding aide
jokes.explanation: 'Great' — one word, maximum economy, complete lie, perfect Selina. The single-word understatement in the most obviously 'not great' situation is the cleanest possible joke delivery.

jokes.text: Jesus, what the fuck happened to you? / Shh, listen, listen. The president is resigning. Selina is gonna be president.
jokes.setup: Selina emerges from the bathroom looking disheveled (blood? chaos?) — Amy's alarmed reaction
jokes.punchline: Selina ignores the question entirely and leads with the historic news, leaving Amy's question hanging
jokes.explanation: The gap between Amy's horrified reaction to Selina's appearance and Selina's complete indifference to it — prioritizing news over hygiene — is character-perfect. The juxtaposition is the joke.

jokes.text: POTUS is resigning. Selina's president. / Fuck off! / POTUS is gonna resign. Selina's gonna be president. / Oh, shit.
jokes.setup: The news ripples through the team — each person hearing it for the first time
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck off!' as the authentic first reaction to learning your boss is becoming President of the United States
jokes.explanation: 'Fuck off!' as the response to historic political news is perfect — it's authentic political staffer shock, dismissing the news as impossible before accepting it. The cascade of people being told and the escalating reactions ('Oh, shit.') is well-structured. 'Fuck off!' is the best possible single word reaction.

jokes.text: Oh, shit. / I was gonna raise ducks.
jokes.setup: Mike processes the news that Selina is becoming president — meaning his dream of leaving for a quieter life is over
jokes.punchline: 'I was gonna raise ducks' — the dream dies in four words, referencing his earlier fantasy
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best joke. Four words, maximum economy, perfect callback, genuine emotion, and complete character summary. 'I was gonna raise ducks' is both the funniest and saddest line in the episode — a tiny dream crushed by historic events, delivered with devastating understatement. The past tense ('was gonna') does everything. The callback to the duck fantasy earns it fully. This is the kind of joke that earns 9+ — memorable, quotable, structurally perfect, and impossible to attribute to any other character.

jokes.text: Oh, Rye Harbor. Oh, that's lovely. So you had a safe arrival, which means that Lady Luck was with you. / Our journey was like a horrible dream. / Mm, right. / At one time, we hid in a well for five days.
jokes.setup: Selina has returned to the Syrian refugees and is making cheerful small talk about their arrival in 'lovely' Rye Harbor
jokes.punchline: Fadi corrects her ('like a horrible dream') and reveals they hid in a well for five days — total tonal mismatch between Selina's breezy tourist framing and actual refugee trauma
jokes.explanation: Selina's tourist-brochure framing of a refugee family's harrowing journey ('Lady Luck was with you') against the reality of five days in a well is excellent dark comedy. The specificity of the well is important — it's not vague suffering but grimly concrete.

jokes.text: Um, it's happening in 48 hours.
jokes.setup: Selina is mid-sentence with the Syrian refugees when Kent interrupts with the presidential timeline
jokes.punchline: Kent's interruption of genuine refugee storytelling with logistics — 48 hours — while they're sitting with people who survived five days in a well
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'five days in a well' and '48 hours' (presidential timeline) is sharp — the contrast between the scale of one number versus the other. Kent's interruption is tonally brutal and perfect.

jokes.text: Okay, I gotta go, but, gosh, good luck with all of your terrible troubles and, um, welcome to America.
jokes.setup: Selina must abruptly leave the Syrian refugees now that she knows she's becoming President in 48 hours
jokes.punchline: 'Good luck with all of your terrible troubles' — an exquisitely inadequate farewell to people who survived war, wells, and refugee journeys
jokes.explanation: 'Good luck with all of your terrible troubles' is a masterpiece of inadequacy — simultaneously empathetic in intent and completely useless in practice. 'Welcome to America' as a presidential sign-off to people who hid in a well for five days is darkly perfect. The entire speech is Selina's political reflex running on empty. High quotability, very high character integration. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Winner, winner, winner, winner, winner. / Winner, winner, winner.
jokes.setup: Selina has extracted herself from the community center and the news has fully landed
jokes.punchline: Selina chanting 'Winner' repeatedly to herself/team — the absurd reduction of 'I'm becoming President' to a sports chant
jokes.explanation: Selina's processing of becoming president via a chicken-dinner chant is character-perfect — the grandiosity is both genuine and slightly ridiculous. The repetition is the joke.

jokes.text: Winner, winner, winner, winner, winner. Winner, winner, winner.
jokes.setup: Selina is with her core team after learning she'll be president
jokes.punchline: Selina's barely-contained celebration reduced to just the word 'winner' repeated — pure unfiltered ego
jokes.explanation: The raw egotism of 'winner winner winner' — no context, no substance, just the label — perfectly captures Selina's motivations. The repetition is the joke.

jokes.text: And, Mike, I need you. / Yes, you do.
jokes.setup: Selina is assembling her team for the presidential transition
jokes.punchline: Mike's completely unselfconscious 'Yes, you do' — agreeing with his own indispensability with no irony
jokes.explanation: Mike's 'Yes, you do' is perfectly Mike — no false modesty, no irony, just genuine belief that he is needed. The joke is his complete sincerity in a moment where self-deprecation would be more appropriate.

jokes.text: And, Gary... / Yeah? / I need you to stay.
jokes.setup: Selina is assembling her transition team — everyone is being summoned
jokes.punchline: Gary, who has been at Selina's side for her entire career, is being left behind — asked to 'stay' to manage the campaign as she goes to become President
jokes.explanation: This is simultaneously the funniest and most heartbreaking joke in the episode. Gary has just had a nosebleed from excitement about Selina becoming president, has carried her bags and served her every need for years, was in the bathroom with her for the most historic moment of her life — and she asks him to stay behind with the campaign. The devastation is on his face and in the 'What?' The cruelty is completely unselfconscious on Selina's part, which is what makes it so funny and so dark. Perfect character integration for both. Justifies 8.7.

jokes.text: This is better than any of the sex I've ever had. / I mean, almost any of it.
jokes.setup: Dan is reveling in the news that Selina is becoming president
jokes.punchline: He compares it to sex, then hedges with 'almost any of it' — a quick walk-back implying some specific sexual memory competes with this
jokes.explanation: The hedge 'almost any of it' is what makes this — the correction implies a specific encounter that would still beat the presidency, which is a very Dan character detail. His self-regard and libido competing for internal rankings is perfectly him.

jokes.text: And then tomorrow, you take a commercial flight back to DC and then we can talk inauguration shoes.
jokes.setup: Selina is consoling Gary about being left behind
jokes.punchline: She dangles 'inauguration shoes' as the reward — reducing the presidency to footwear choices
jokes.explanation: Gary is being placated with inauguration shoes — perfectly capturing both Selina's shallow priorities and her manipulation of Gary through his devotion to her appearance. 'Commercial flight' versus presumably the presidential jet is also a quiet additional joke.

jokes.text: I feel sick, but it's just in my neck, you know?
jokes.setup: Ben is processing the extraordinary news that Selina will be President
jokes.punchline: 'I feel sick, but it's just in my neck' — an anatomically bizarre description of emotion that somehow captures something real about stress
jokes.explanation: 'Sick but just in my neck' is a wonderfully strange description of political anxiety — it's physically specific and emotionally true in a way that makes no logical sense. Very Ben: stoic, weary, expressing massive events through minor physical complaints. High originality, perfect character integration.

jokes.text: Wow, only 44 other senior press advisors have ever felt like this. / That's right, yeah. We're the chosen ones, bud.
jokes.setup: Mike and Dan are savoring the moment of working for a new president
jokes.punchline: Mike frames it in terms of the exclusive club of presidential press advisors; Dan confirms 'we're the chosen ones' — with all the self-importance that entails
jokes.explanation: Mike quantifying the historic moment with a statistic ('44 other press advisors') is a very Mike way to process emotion — turning a feeling into a data point. 'The chosen ones' from Dan is appropriately messianic and self-serving.

jokes.text: Wow, it really is all about you, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Mike and Dan are celebrating their own historic roles as the team processes becoming a presidential staff
jokes.punchline: Amy's withering observation that in the moment of Selina becoming president, the men have made it about themselves
jokes.explanation: Amy's dry observation is a perfect Amy beat — she's the one who notices everyone's self-absorption. The line works because it's directed at multiple people who've been making the moment about themselves.

jokes.text: Is this... is this soup vegetarian? / I think it's vegan. / Ah, nice. / So maybe one or two types of beans? / I wouldn't know. I don't make the soup. / Oh, maybe three, actually.
jokes.setup: The team is at a soup kitchen; Ben is getting soup while processing the news that Selina won't be president (the announcement was 'with immediate effect' — meaning POTUS already announced)
jokes.punchline: Ben calmly inquires about soup bean varieties while everything around him collapses — 'Oh, maybe three, actually' delivered as if this is the most important information
jokes.explanation: Ben's detached soup inquiry while Selina's presidential dream implodes is perfect Ben characterization — his coping mechanism is total compartmentalization. 'I think it's vegan. Ah, nice.' as a response to professional annihilation is beautifully deadpan. The escalating specificity about bean count is absurdist.

jokes.text: Someone has just flown two planes into my career.
jokes.setup: The team learns POTUS has already made the announcement — with 'immediate effect' meaning Selina does NOT become president
jokes.punchline: Dan's 9/11 metaphor for his career destruction — 'two planes into my career'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The 9/11 metaphor for personal career disaster is shockingly dark and completely Dan — the scale of the reference versus the selfishness of the application is the joke. 'Two planes' (not one — maximum devastation) and 'my career' (not America, not Selina — himself) is precise character writing. Maximum economy, high originality. Justifies 8.6 easily.

jokes.text: I don't understand this, 'cause yesterday we were all doing jokes about the box, now she's commander-in-chief. How is this happened?
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to process the rapid reversal from crate jokes to the presidency to now... not the presidency
jokes.punchline: 'Yesterday we were doing jokes about the box' — perfectly summarizing the episode's tonal journey; the grammatical error ('How is this happened') adds to Mike's frazzled state
jokes.explanation: A meta moment that works as genuine character comedy — Mike's bewilderment at the episode's own plot is a soft meta-joke. 'Doing jokes about the box' is a perfect summary of where they were 20 minutes ago. 'How is this happened?' grammar collapse is a nice touch of panic.

jokes.text: It's my greatest joy just to stand by her every day, just to be by her side.
jokes.setup: Gary is being interviewed (presumably for a documentary or reporter) about working for Selina
jokes.punchline: The audience knows Gary has just been told to stay behind while Selina goes to become President — this sincere statement of devotion is immediately undercut by her abandonment of him
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is devastating — Gary's most sincere statement of loyalty ('my greatest joy') comes right after Selina left him behind. The audience knows what Gary is too devoted to fully process. The cringe-comedy of his unconditional love for someone who just abandoned him at her greatest moment is very high character integration. Justifies 8.2.

jokes.text: Buy yourself a gun, Mom, because America's gonna have to shoot its way out of this.
jokes.setup: JJ is watching news coverage of the presidential situation with his mother
jokes.punchline: JJ's political assessment delivered as maternal advice: buy a gun
jokes.explanation: 'Buy yourself a gun, Mom, because America's gonna have to shoot its way out of this' is a great dark JJ line — it sounds like political analysis but is really just nihilistic despair delivered to his mother. High economy, good character integration, very quotable.

jokes.text: Does she often leave you behind? / No, this is the first time.
jokes.setup: A reporter asks Gary about being left behind
jokes.punchline: Gary's answer is technically true ('first time') — but the audience knows this is the most important time she's ever left him behind, making his answer both accurate and heartbreaking
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'No, this is the first time' works on multiple levels: it's a sad statement of fact, it implies Selina's total reliance on Gary until now, and it lands the gut punch that she's leaving him for the biggest moment. Perfect economy. The question is also accidentally cruel, and Gary answers with complete dignity. High character integration.

jokes.text: Oh, I didn't get any salad. / Oh, God, Mom. No, make this stop. / Oh, okay, okay. / No, don't turn it off, Mom.
jokes.setup: JJ and his mother are watching the news; JJ's mother notices she doesn't have her salad
jokes.punchline: JJ's desperate 'No, make this stop' — a cry at the news coverage — followed by his reversal 'No, don't turn it off' when his mother takes him literally
jokes.explanation: The mother's focus on her missing salad during a political crisis, and JJ's cry 'make this stop' being interpreted literally (as 'turn off the TV'), is good family-comedy misunderstanding. His immediate reversal is a nice beat.

jokes.text: Whoa, this must be what it feels like to be happy.
jokes.setup: JJ watches as the presidency falls through for Selina — meaning the political landscape reopens for someone like him
jokes.punchline: 'This must be what it feels like to be happy' — said as if happiness is an unfamiliar sensation requiring identification
jokes.explanation: 'This must be what it feels like to be happy' suggests that JJ has been so uniformly miserable that happiness requires diagnostic identification. The past tense of his existential suffering is implied in one line. Maximum economy, strong character integration for someone we've watched be repeatedly humiliated this episode.

jokes.text: I'm gonna be the fucking president.
jokes.setup: The episode's final scene — Selina alone, processing the reversal back to her becoming president (or the initial news sinking in)
jokes.punchline: The episode closes on Selina saying 'I'm gonna be the fucking president' — the profanity and delivery making the awe and disbelief perfectly clear
jokes.explanation: The final line is a perfect episode button. Six words, maximum economy, it serves simultaneously as punchline, character summary, and emotional catharsis. 'The fucking president' — the profanity is what sells it, the awe-in-obscenity that Selina processes every big emotion through. This is the culmination of the episode's tonal journey from crate jokes to this. It earns its 8.7 by being the ideal last line.

jokes.text: I'm gonna be the fucking president.
jokes.setup: Episode's closing moment — Selina alone, absorbing the reality
jokes.punchline: The profanity-laced presidential declaration as the final note — simultaneously triumphant and completely undignified
jokes.explanation: The perfect closing line for the episode and Selina's character. 'I'm gonna be the fucking president' captures the triumph, the vulgarity, and the complete inappropriateness of how she expresses it. 'Fucking' is doing enormous character work — it's not presidential, it's Selina. Strong closer.

tmdb_overview: With Thornhill ahead in the polls, Selina and her staff discuss how to make her more "folksy." In need of a job, Jonah meets with Bill Ericsson. Selina has an interview with pretentious journalist Quincy Carter.
insight: Wordplay carries a middling episode as cringe takes backseat to character comedy.
